speaker: interviewee
text: [COUGH]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that you heard that your mother and your brother were in a collective farm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah at [NON-ENGLISH]. So I just picked myself up with a couple of things. I had some like a pot. That's all my [NON-ENGLISH] was, a pot and the-- something else, I don't know what. And I went to the station. But I couldn't get a ticket. You had to have a special paper from the government.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow I managed [INAUDIBLE] and I managed to go on with the train on top of the roof. On top of the roof of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train, I rode the back-- not buses, like these old train wagons, you know, with little chimneys. I went on top of that, you know. And it just-- that way you went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that take?

speaker: interviewee
text: A day and a couple a nights. And you went there and the [? Komstadt ?] station, you're first going down and you hide. And when the train starts moving, you go up again. You know, that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only--

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't think. When you're so young, you have really don't think. You just has a-- you have to do something, you do it and that's all. You don't think about the consequences, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you the only person on the top of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was a lot, all kind of guys there, all kind. There was even thieves there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thieves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thieves on top of the train. And as they come to you, they ask you for money. You know that. But before I left, the guys told me, watch out. If you're on top of the roof, those guys searching you everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you got to do, you got a sweater, put in this-- you know, what do you call it? In the seam. Put there, you got to roll it up and put it in the seam around you. So the money, if they go like that, they don't find anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which I did, really. I put in the seam. I had a few, a few hundred rubles, you know. And I put it-- but they came and they did search you. And at night when it was [INAUDIBLE], you may have-- you're afraid you'll fall asleep, so you tied yourself to the little chimney. And that way you slept, you know. And I came-- I, finally I went to the destination. I came there. I had to walk awful far to this farm, you know. And the rail, it only goes to the city there. And what I saw there, I fainted. When I saw there my mother and my brother-- it was a room. I wouldn't put pigs. I swear to God. It was a dark-- no windows, no nothing. It's just like a stable, a small stable, not anything. And they were there. My mother looked very bad, very sick. And ate-- you didn't-- they gave-- hardly any food they gave them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they end up there? Why were they there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They I took them probably with the trains. In other words, they spread them up. You go there. You go there, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was a father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father wasn't there. And I, uh, we stayed just maybe I said a couple of days. And my mom became very ill, very ill. So-- it wasn't easy to get to the, to the office from this collective farm. And I thought if I needed a horse or something, somebody to bring my mom to the hospital because she's very ill. It took a long time, but they brought a horse and buggy. Took my mommy and my brother, took my mom on the horse and buggy and went to the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't how many it was, about 30 kilometers maybe to the city. I don't remember how much. 25? I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came there. And me, I am on one side, on my mom's side, and my brother on the other side. And we came to the city. We didn't know it. When the doctor came out to my mom, he said, your momma has died already an hour ago, maybe more. We didn't even know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't know that my mother was dead on this carriage there, on the buggy there. So what to do? We took her into the hospital. And then I called-- somehow, I found out about a Jewish-- Cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Settlement there. They had those [? Muhara ?] Jews, you know that-- and they managed to make a, a funeral for my mom. Nobody was there, just me and my brother, a couple of those guys there from the-- we took her into the cemetery. And that's it. It was [NON-ENGLISH]. I don't remember the name there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] means-- it's like a small-- it's not a city. It's like a, like a small town there, you know. And nothing else to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom-- in the cemetery my brother and I said, what we going to do now? Let's go to the city, to Samarkand. It was Samarkand there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked, I don't know, a couple nights and a day. We came to the city. And my brother said, well, here we're going to separate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. He don't tell me. He has somebody maybe there in the city. I had no idea. He was not a very old man, either. He was maybe 20, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I let him go. And I went back to the, to Leninabad, you know. He went there. Of course, I never saw him again in my life. Your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never saw him again. Never. Such a good guy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I went back there. And a few months later, things get worse. Somebody told that my father, they know where is my father is-- in a collective farm, but in the same area somewhere but in a different collective farm there. I went back.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the train again?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I go by-- you cannot walk. And not by buggy, either, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: On top of the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: And certainly not a plane. On top, what else can you do it? Nobody gives you a paper to go buy a ticket to go. And this was a much worse trip, much worse trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when I came to one station, I couldn't go back on the train. There was a couple of guys. That   room was  cold-- a couple of other guys. And we had to wait at the next train, the morning or some other, quite a few hours. It was in the evening, maybe 11:00. And at that time they, they took-- or they talked me into to go with them to the sideline is those boxcars. You know, trains standing still. They don't go nowhere. We can take a nap there, you know, on those benches and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was stupid. I went with them there. It was two guys and a girl. And when I woke up in the morning, they took away my shoes. I was sleeping, and they took my shoes away, what I had, the little thing there, the, the pot and all that. I finished. I don't know what can you say? Sit and cry? What can I do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning, I went at the station somehow and went back on a train. I'm going on a train for a whole day. And the evening, I saw a guy with my pot. It's not funny. Now, it looks like funny-- but my pot, you know what I had, and my shoes. And the train was moving. I went to him. I had a knife like that, called a khanjar. This was in Asia. They kept those khanjars. And I said, I want my pot. I want my shoes, otherwise you're dead. And I stood true, so help me god. And he said, OK, OK, OK. He gave me the, the, my shoes. He gave me my pot. I say-- and I went to that one, jumped to another wagon. And I went. And I came to a destination. I went to there to this collective farm. I saw my dad. He had a beard, you know, and all that. But he was in a much better situation than my mom and my brother was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bukhara somewhere. I can't remember the name. In Bukhara. By Bukhara. This was by Samarkand. This was by Bukhara.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of year was this? What time of year? It was, I'd say, in [NON-ENGLISH] though, it was cool, maybe in the autumn. I'm not sure. Because in the evening it was pretty cold. It doesn't go so cold there because in Asia it's always warm, just like California. You know the weather. But we talked. We talked a couple of days. All kind of from the family things, you know. Did you tell him about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't know, shall I say this? My mother and my father didn't get along too good, too good. I don't know. Does it have to be included in this conversation or not?

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. You can go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't. They weren't. And we talk and that, and-- I was a couple of days there. And then he had a woman there. And I left. I left. I went back to Leninabad. In between time, when I told you, I learned this trade to become a driver, a truck driver. And I was driving there. And I was working for a big state company. My boss was a Russian Jew. He was like, here, shall we say, state controller. That's, that was his job, state controller. And he used to drive around to the, to the big-- it's not farm but collective points that they-- they used to bring all the grain from rice and everything-- they used to bring there. And from there, there used to be this-- to the military, to the whatever that, the government. And he was a controller. He used to check if the everything's in order, you  know. And I, the driver and I-- I stayed over there with this driver-- I used to go with him, you know. This was-- we didn't have no car, but we drove-- it was a three-ton truck. And you go over there. The one [INAUDIBLE] was a very nice, a very nice man. He really treat me like, like a son. He was very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have any sense during this time of how the war was progressing or what was happening to the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we heard a little bit on the radio. But it said it was a very big fight, you know. And then it was the fight in Stalingrad. They fought house by house and all that. But it didn't much-- it wasn't much that-- the Russians didn't give you any news on the radio, you know. Everything was, was a secret, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had no sense of what was happening to the Jews in Eastern Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not at all. We didn't know about Nazi Germany, about the-- about the Jewish concentration camp. I didn't know nothing about that stuff at all. And then there a time, when I said no-- a couple of years, probably. I think it was a times at the 19-- end of '45, right beginning of '46, was it that the Jews-- not the Jews actually, all the Pollacks could go back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was after the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they told us there.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is when you were driving the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that the war is over. And all these people now, for they came during the war from Poland, from Ukraine there, can go back home. You have to register, you know, and all that. And they give you like a boxcar train, no [INAUDIBLE] train, but a boxcar train. They give you, and you just went back. A lot of people speculate there. The older people, they make a lot of money. They bought gold, and they made wooden boxes. And they drill holes, and they put the gold. Or start nails, put a little nail on top, all kind of manipulations. But I, as a young man, I didn't know much about it. I was happy. I had some clothes, good on. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Home? No, I didn't want to stop at my town. I know that my town, where the German were, what I'm going-- I know nobody is there. My immediate family, was nobody there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went all the way to, you call, Lvov, Lvov, you know. And from there-- this was the border. The Russians and the Pollacks, you understand, was divided when the Germans with the Russians, they divided Poland. So this was Ukraine. And then was supposedly Poland was the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over there, we just passed by, passed this, and we went to, to Poland. Poland, I went-- you know, I didn't know what to do. Anyway, I went to Krakow. To Krakow, I didn't know where to go, or what to do. So I went to [NON-ENGLISH] to the party there, you know. Was a lot of guys already there, or gals, and boys, and younger, and older, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that group you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a party, a Zionist organization. I guess it was founded already, maybe from America. I don't know, from everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now was it-- where was it that you did eventually learn what had happened to the Jews in Eastern Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: This, when I came to, to, to Poland. You know, right after war, I learned all about it in Poland, you know. But I didn't stay in Poland long. Was in Poland after the war-- was still fights, was still persecuting Jews after the war. You understand? I didn't want to stay in Poland much. So I tried to get the border, across the border to Czechoslovakia, Czechoslovakia to Austria, from Austria to West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ended up in West Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ended up in West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at Ulm. Matter of fact, before that, when I was in Krakow, in Krakow, I find out somehow that my dad lived through the war there. And he's in Liegnitz, Liegnitz in Poland. And I put myself together. And I went to him, to see him. I went there. He lived in an apartment with some woman there, you know. I talked to him and all that. And I saw that I was in the way. I wasn't anymore 10 years old. I was in the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back to the [NON-ENGLISH], where I was there. And from there, we went to Weissenberg. And Ger-- it was Poland, Germany there, on the border there. I went over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I guess from there I went to, uh, to Czechoslovakia and Austria and then to West Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in West Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years, I guess. A little over two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there-- first when I came, I went same year to Ulm, to a DP camp. And between time, my dad was there in the camp already.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad was in the camp. He was a, a [NON-ENGLISH], on, on the-- what do you call this? Not from not any party, he just was a dependent. He was a-- some in the committee from as a independent. So this day he could manage to get me a job as a policeman in the camp, in the DP camp. So when I was there-- What did you do as a policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: What you do, you stay by the gate and watch. But matter of fact, one was interesting. In 1946, just at the end, when I was just in the camp, Ben-Gurion came to speak there in the camp. He used to go a lot of those camps in West Germany. And he spoke to the people to-- mostly young people-- people to go to Israel, to those, those to Palestine. And I was fortunate enough, I met him basically because I was just like a bodyguard in the camp. You know, he was his own people had. There were a few policeman from the camp, and I met him. He had a few speeches he made there and all that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was there. There was a friend of mine. I met a guy there from Belgium. No, he was a Polish guy. And he said to me, how about go-- we go to Belgium? But to go there, first we've got to work in the mines. I said, the mines? You know, in under ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, I have enough trouble to live on top of the ground. I don't want to go to underground. Not me, I had enough. I have enough of that. Well, I guess he went. And I stayed there and somehow got connection. I went to Munich. And I worked for the office JDC, for the Joint. You know, the American, um, organization to help Jews after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was their-- I was like a patrolman. What shall I tell you? It was-- it took over a building from the Germans, big, big building was [? a. ?] And over there was living this, the, the American MP lived there, and the-- and the worker from the military, the PO, or the post office, from the military. And of course, those guys [INAUDIBLE] who worked for the-- for JDD-- for the DC-- for the Joint. I worked there. I had a good job. Compared to what I had before, I had a good job. I have a nice apartment, you know, there. And I make a little bit black marketeering there with the, with the, with the soldiers and all that. I had it all right. But in 1948, on the end-- I wanted to go before. It didn't-- didn't work it out. I wanted to go before to Israel, to Palestine through Austria. I didn't work it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So end of '48, just about beginning of '49, actually it was in February, I went to-- to, uh-- Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to Palestine. I said [INAUDIBLE] Israel. With the [NON-ENGLISH], with the ship there, it was awful. You know, we slept way, three floors down, all that. But we came there. And still wasn't-- so was still the war a little bit going on. Even they finished the war, you were fighting with the Arabs still, anyways.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I came there. And right away, from the ship right away to the camp, you know. And in a couple of days they show us how to shoot, buh, buh, buh. And this I was in the army there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there they put you in a different camp, you know. I spent 30 months in the Israeli army. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In a way, I was happy because I didn't go to the Russia. I could go to the Russian army if I would make myself younger and all that. I probably could go to the Russian army. But I went there. I spent 30 months in the army. And I came out of the army. And working, from the beginning it was very hard to get jobs. But I worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I find a job in the, from the Joint in Israel. I find a job as a-- in charge as a department and a warehouse. You know other like-- how do  you call this? A supervisor?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working there. The pay, the pay wasn't too bad. Too good, but it wasn't too bad. I was working there. I had my-- used to give good rations, this, this, all kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you were traveling from Russia to Germany to Israel, how were you able to communicate in all these different countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: Communicate?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn all the languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, you have one language is good all over, Esperanto. That's Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yiddish. Yiddish, you can communicate with everybody. And of course, I know Polish. I know it from before. And I know in Russian. I know it Polish. And you come to Germany-- I must say, I was very quick in languages, very quick, you know. In Germany you spoke Yiddish. I thought that, I though that it, it's German. But somehow I communicate with the German. And I learned the language pretty fast and pretty good.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then Hebrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hebrew is in Israel. I know a little bit [BACKGROUND NOISE] from my bar mitzvah days. But [BACKGROUND NOISE] I learned Hebrew in the army. You know, I was in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did it feel being in the Israeli army? [BREAK IN TAPE] We can go to another. I was asking you how it felt to fight in the army for Israel after having been in Russia during the war.

words.text: [BREAK IN TAPE] We can go to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the most wonderful feeling a person can ever have.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to serve in a-- in a-- an army it you don't believe it, or the Russian, or in the Pollacks. You know, I was happy to serve the Israeli army as a Jew and a survivor from all this Holocaust and everything else, what it was against Jews. And I was happy. I was proud. And matter of fact, I was finished, I was a sergeant in the Israeli army. And I was very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you move to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America, I came here in December of '57. I could go-- could maybe become a year before. But I couldn't go out because the war. '56. I had to be in the war, and the '53, and I was in all the wars. And I couldn't get out. Not that I didn't want it. They didn't let me out. Even if I wanted to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got married in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, the married-- I-- when I quit the army in '51. I was working here and there. And then I met my wife in the bus station.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was actually was a friend of hers, which I know it, just as an acquaintance. And she saw me by the bus. She says, Bill-- well, not Bill, that time Zev, they call me in Hebrew-- come over. I says, what are you doing? I had just a package I received from the United States, from my dad. My dad was here in the United States. So I received this package. And she said, you have some coffee in there? I think so. Why don't you come over to my house and make some coffee? So I said, sure, why not? Why don't you meet my friend? That's my wife. And what's her name? Or what was her name then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ali. She never changed her name. So we met. We talk. And I came to her house. We lived in a small, a little small house called the [NON-ENGLISH]. And it's by Holon. And we talked, talked. Then I kind of liked her, so I made up a date. And it started from there on, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you moved here into America in 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved in-- my dad started communicating with me from America, maybe I want to come. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It was very hard. But I managed, managed to get made some papers for me. And I came here on December of '57, alone. My wife couldn't come with me because the paper wasn't big enough collateral to come, my wife with my son, here. So I came by myself. And a year later, when I established myself, my wife came with my little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I know your wife has been interviewed and is going to appear with you here for a minute. Looking back on everything that you experienced, and in the years that have passed since then, is there any message that you would like to leave with people who might be seeing this tape in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: One wish is mine is that those things never happen again. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add before we look at your photographs and you introduce us to your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must tell-- I must say that I didn't say everything what I experienced because there's a lot of stuff that comes to your mind later, and I don't feel like going back, and going back, and going back. Many things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything in particular you would like to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was in trouble. I was raped by natives there, all kind of stuff

speaker: interviewer
text: But where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in, in, in Russia, in Asia there, by the native.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942. All kind of stuff what's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to report to any police?

speaker: interviewee
text: To who? [LAUGHS] Police? People can't realize what does mean Russia, especially those days in a small town like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: You can go from one city to the other and be a new person there with a new pass-- a new paper and everything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when you were traveling by the collective farms?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the city. And one-- another time it happens at-- I was in the movies. It was late in the evening. Later in the evening, we saw the picture-- in the open movie now. Not really had drive-in. Over there is walk-in movies. You walk in. It's outside. You watch the movie. I was sitting with a guy right next to me. And he says-- we start talking. I'm from Europe, buh-buh-buh. OK. Come over to my house. And we'll have some tea, have some-- And I, like was a schmendrick, you know. I went with him. And, you know, the women have no right to coming in the room when you have a guest. And the man has a guest, the woman has no right. If she come in, she has to cover her face and everything. You come in and give us the room. And he's starting to come up on me, and I just dumped everything, and I run like hell. I didn't know where I run, even. In the small streets, like you see in the Arab cities, a small street. And I just run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was also in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Yeah. What can I tell you? So many stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is it that you think, despite all the things that happened to you, that you were able to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know, really. You know, go with a mother-- next to your mother, and sitting next to her, and not even know she's dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know she was dead. We came there to the, by the hospital there. And the guy came out-- the doctor-- and looked, says-- said, you guys, what's the matter with you? She's dead, right here, over an hour, maybe two hours. You couldn't estimate those days, not like now, you know. [COUGHS] How can you feel? I mean, you know. A lot of time, my kids can not understand me, either. I have a little bit, how should I tell you? Backfire, back  memory-- a lot of time I get aggravated. I get nervous.

speaker: interviewee
text: They don't know why. And I don't know why, either. I don't know. Upsetting, you know, and all that. My kids are mostly-- their two Americans. One was born in Israel, but he came here, he was 3 or 4 years old. One was born here in L.A. you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to put on a new tape so that you can show us the photos.
